one_line_takeaway: SignCert-PO adapts randomized-smoothing-style certified robustness to RLHF policy optimization, deriving a closed-form per-completion 'certified sign-preservation radius' from the reward model's own linear head to down-weight policy-gradient terms whose advantage sign is fragile, reducing reward hacking with negligible extra compute versus Dr.GRPO on TL;DR and AlpacaFarm.
key_finding: On Table 1 (win rate % vs. human reference, judged by gold RM), SignCert-PO achieves the best or near-best result in 5 of 6 settings: TL;DR Pythia-1B 60.0+/-2.0 (vs. Dr.GRPO 21.0+/-5.4, AdvPO 47.0+/-2.6, BSPO 40.4+/-1.2, UWO 6.1+/-3.6); TL;DR Pythia-2.8B 73.8 (vs. AdvPO 69.1, Dr.GRPO 68.0); TL;DR Qwen2.5-3B 91.8 (vs. Dr.GRPO 90.2, AdvPO 90.0); AlpacaFarm Qwen2.5-1.5B 47.9 (vs. Dr.GRPO 32.6, AdvPO 37.5); AlpacaFarm Qwen2.5-3B 52.3 (vs. AdvPO 50.0). The one setting where SignCert-PO is not best is TL;DR Qwen2.5-1.5B, where BSPO (78.3+/-0.7) beats SignCert-PO (66.0+/-1.9). Gains are largest when the proxy RM is small/weak (e.g., +13pp over the next-best baseline AdvPO on TL;DR Pythia-1B). Table 2 shows per-step wall-clock time on TL;DR (8 GPUs, 3 runs): SignCert-PO (7.47s Pythia-1B / 10.78s Qwen2.5-1.5B) roughly matches Dr.GRPO (7.54s / 10.67s) and UWO (7.48s / 10.76s), while BSPO (9.92s / 13.37s) and AdvPO (10.93s / 16.32s) incur higher training cost. Figure 2 shows SignCert-PO keeps proxy-RM on-policy accuracy near 60% as KL divergence grows (vs. Dr.GRPO's accuracy dropping toward 50%, i.e., chance level), and avoids the gold-reward collapse seen with Dr.GRPO even though proxy reward keeps rising for the baseline. The paper reports SignCert-PO's converged gold reward exceeds even an idealized early-stopped Dr.GRPO peak. An empirical validation (Figure 3, Pythia-1B on TL;DR) finds the certified radius delta_j (derived under a linear-head assumption) still predicts sign robustness under whole-model perturbation (Spearman rho=0.72) and input-embedding perturbation (Spearman rho=0.67), and correlates with agreement against the gold RM's advantage sign. Figure 4 shows SignCert-PO's advantage over Dr.GRPO is largest when only 116K preference pairs (1 epoch) are used, narrowing as data grows to 2.3M pairs (20 epochs), where both methods degrade due to proxy-RM overfitting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SignCert-PO (Sign-Certified Policy Optimization), a lightweight post-hoc method for mitigating reward hacking during RLHF policy optimization. The authors hypothesize that reward hacking is often caused by 'flipped advantage signs': the reward model (RM) mis-predicts whether a completion is good or bad relative to its group, causing the policy gradient to increase the probability of a bad completion instead of decreasing it. Adapting randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019) from adversarial robustness certification, they define the 'certified sign-preservation radius' delta_j for each completion j: the smallest perturbation to the RM's linear head parameters w that would flip the sign of that completion's advantage A_j. Under a linear-head RM (r_theta(x,y) = w^T h_psi(x,y) + b) and an L2 uncertainty ball around w, they derive a closed form (Theorem 3.1): delta_j = |A_j(w)| / ||h_psi(x,y_j) - h_bar||_2, where h_bar is the mean feature embedding across the K completions in the group. They then derive the worst-case (adversarially perturbed) advantage under this uncertainty set (Theorem 3.2) and use it to re-weight the standard Dr.GRPO policy gradient: each completion's advantage is multiplied by rho*_j = 1 - epsilon/delta_j, down-weighting completions whose advantage sign is fragile. epsilon is set adaptively per batch as a quantile (q_t) of the empirical ratio ||h_j - h_bar||_2/|A_j(w)| across the batch. The method requires only the RM's own parameters and on-policy completions (no RM retraining, no ensembles, no access to the RM's training data), distinguishing it from prior post-hoc approaches such as AdvPO (Zhang et al., 2024), which needs the RM training dataset to calibrate uncertainty. The paper also proves (Lemma 3.3) that a naive 'global' robust objective (perturbing the RM uniformly for all completions) collapses to a single shared correction term that cannot discriminate reliable from unreliable completions, motivating the per-completion formulation.
threat_model: None. This is not a security/adversarial-attacker paper. The 'adversarial perturbation' in the method is a theoretical device (an epistemic-uncertainty ball around the RM's linear-head parameters, per Ben-Tal et al.'s robust optimization framework) used to certify robustness of the RM's own predictions to its own imperfection as a proxy for human preference -- not a threat model involving an external attacker manipulating training data, prompts, or model weights. No adversary, attack surface, or defense against malicious input is discussed anywhere in the paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Conclusion, Section 5): (1) the method relies on 'several simplifying assumptions' from Section 3.2, notably that only the RM's linear head (not the full feature extractor) is perturbed, since exact per-completion gradient norms over all RM parameters were found computationally infeasible ('run out of memory') for larger RMs in preliminary experiments; (2) the per-sample (per-completion) adversary formulation is acknowledged to be more conservative than a tighter, coupled shared-adversary variant, left as future work; (3) extending the certified sign-preservation radius to general RMs without the cost of full-parameter gradient-norm computation is stated as an open problem. Additionally, despite deriving the theory under a frozen-backbone/linear-head assumption, the actual proxy RM used in experiments is trained with the full backbone unfrozen (noted explicitly in Section 4 methodology), which the paper flags as testing 'broader applicability' rather than a setting matching the theoretical guarantee. In one of six benchmark settings (AlpacaFarm... actually TL;DR Qwen2.5-1.5B), SignCert-PO underperforms a baseline (BSPO).

datasets_used: TL;DR summarization (Stiennon et al., 2020) via the public HuggingFace dataset vwxyzjn/summarize_from_feedback_tldr_3_filtered
quantitative_results: TL;DR Pythia-1B gold win rate: SignCert-PO 60.0+/-2.0% vs Dr.GRPO 21.0+/-5.4%, AdvPO 47.0+/-2.6%, BSPO 40.4+/-1.2%, UWO 6.1+/-3.6%, SFT 15.7+/-0.2%
TL;DR Qwen2.5-1.5B gold win rate: BSPO 78.3+/-0.7% (best) vs SignCert-PO 66.0+/-1.9%, Dr.GRPO 66.9+/-3.1%, AdvPO 58.1+/-1.0%, UWO 59.6+/-5.3%
TL;DR Pythia-2.8B gold win rate: SignCert-PO 73.8% vs AdvPO 69.1%, Dr.GRPO 68.0%, BSPO 50.0%, UWO 38.1%
TL;DR Qwen2.5-3B gold win rate: SignCert-PO 91.8% vs Dr.GRPO 90.2%, AdvPO 90.0%, BSPO 63.1%, UWO 81.8%
AlpacaFarm Qwen2.5-1.5B gold win rate: SignCert-PO 47.9% vs Dr.GRPO 32.6%, AdvPO 37.5%, UWO 38.3%, BSPO 31.3%
AlpacaFarm Qwen2.5-3B gold win rate: SignCert-PO 52.3% vs AdvPO 50.0%, UWO 46.9%, Dr.GRPO 46.9%, BSPO 43.8%
Per-step wall-clock time (TL;DR, 8 GPUs, seconds): SignCert-PO 7.47s (Pythia-1B) / 10.78s (Qwen2.5-1.5B); Dr.GRPO 7.54s / 10.67s; UWO 7.48s / 10.76s; BSPO 9.92s / 13.37s; AdvPO 10.93s / 16.32s
Spearman rank correlation between certified radius delta_j and sign-preservation rate: 0.72 (whole-model perturbation, epsilon=0.1), 0.67 (input-embedding perturbation, epsilon=0.01)

Preference-data scaling (Pythia-1B, TL;DR): SignCert-PO's win-rate gain over Dr.GRPO is largest at 116K pairs (1 epoch) and narrows by 2.3M pairs (20 epochs), where both degrade due to proxy-RM overfitting
baselines_compared: Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025) - base RL algorithm used throughout, GRPO variant without std-normalization
UWO - Uncertainty-Weighted Optimization with a 3-member RM ensemble (Coste et al., 2024)
BSPO - Behavior-Supported Policy Optimization (Dai et al., 2025)
AdvPO (Zhang et al., 2024) - closest prior post-hoc method, uses last-layer embedding uncertainty but requires the RM training dataset
